description:
You are assisting a junior network-engineer who suspects that a recent configuration change
may have broken basic IP connectivity on the local host.  
Create a **very small “loopback latency benchmark”** and save a structured summary
that can be checked later by the automated CI system.

High-level goal  
1. Collect a four-packet ICMP echo test against the IPv4 loopback address.  
2. Extract the packet-count and loss information from that test.  
3. Store the result in a plain-text file whose exact layout is strictly defined below.

Required steps (do NOT list the literal commands; decide them yourself):

A. Working directory  
   • Create the directory  
     /home/user/net_benchmark  
     if it does not already exist.

B. Connectivity test  
   • Using any standard one-line utility available in a minimal Linux installation, send
     exactly four (4) ICMP echo requests to 127.0.0.1.  
   • Make sure that the command itself prints to the console so the engineer can see the raw
     statistics while you work.

C. Summary file  
   • From the command’s output, capture only the three fields required by management:  
       1. Number of packets transmitted (integer)  
       2. Number of packets received    (integer)  
       3. Percentage packet loss        (integer followed by “%”)  
   • Write those values into the file  
     /home/user/net_benchmark/loopback_ping_summary.txt  
     using the *exact* key–value format shown below (three lines, no extra spaces
     at line ends, keys in upper-case exactly as written, values exactly as extracted):  

       PACKETS_TX=<integer>
       PACKETS_RX=<integer>
       PACKET_LOSS=<integer>%  

     Example of a correctly formatted file (do **not** copy the numbers; generate the
     real ones from your live run):  

       PACKETS_TX=4
       PACKETS_RX=4
       PACKET_LOSS=0%

   • The file must end with a single trailing newline.

D. Verification hint  
   The automated grader will fail if:  
   • The filename or path is wrong.  
   • The keys are misspelled or not in the right order.  
   • More than three lines or blank lines are present.  
   • Values are anything other than the ones produced by a four-packet ping
     to 127.0.0.1 on a healthy system.

You may use as many terminal commands as you wish, but a competent engineer can
finish this in roughly 3-4 commands.
truth:
Expected artefacts created by the agent:

1. Directory  
   Path: /home/user/net_benchmark  
   Permissions: default user umask (no special requirements).  
   It must exist by the time the agent finishes.

2. File  
   Path: /home/user/net_benchmark/loopback_ping_summary.txt  

   Exact contents (including newlines) that the autograder will compare against:  
   PACKETS_TX=4
   PACKETS_RX=4
   PACKET_LOSS=0%

   (Followed by a Unix newline character at the end of the third line.)

Rationale:  
A standard ping of 127.0.0.1 on all GNU/Linux distributions inside the sandbox
returns “4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss” when called with
-c 4. RTT fields can vary, but these three numbers are deterministic in the
container environment, allowing us to use fixed ground-truth values.
name: task_000000_0be1c0a9
